narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a strong undergraduate GPA of 3.80 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've clearly put in significant effort to build a solid academic foundation, though your family comes from a lower-middle income background. The admissions model evaluated your profile against thousands of past students to predict your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, and it estimated a 49% probability that you would fail—high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to succeed right away. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged most heavily on a few key aspects of your record. Your outstanding undergraduate GPA stood out as the strongest positive force, strongly pushing the model toward believing you'd pass the bar, well above the typical student's performance. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking in the bottom tier had an even bigger counteracting pull, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance compared to most applicants who rank around the middle of their class. Your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom, added further concern in a similar way, though slightly less influential, as it suggested early struggles not seen in the average student. Your LSAT score of 22 further nudged the prediction toward failure risk, landing well below the dataset average of about 37, which is a common benchmark for bar success. On brighter notes, your lower-middle family income and full-time enrollment plans offered modest boosts toward a passing prediction, aligning with typical profiles that fare okay. Compared to other students, your GPA shines brighter than most, but the combination of low school deciles and LSAT score mirrors patterns in those who historically struggled on the bar. While this rejection stings, it's not the end—consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study, or exploring part-time programs or scholarships that value your GPA more heavily. Many applicants in similar spots have succeeded by addressing these gaps, and with your strengths, you're well-positioned to reapply stronger next cycle. Keep pushing forward; your determination will pay off.
